perturbation.original_text: The receiving party acknowledges that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement, and the disclosing party will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies will not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement but will be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity to the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party acknowledges that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement, and the disclosing party will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies will not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement but will be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity to the disclosing party. This is further related to section 1.
perturbation.explanation: Remedies are generally outlined in their own section. Referring the reader to Section 1 muddies the clear rights and remedies available.
perturbation.law_explanation: By adding 'This is further related to section 1' to the remedies section, this modification creates uncertainty about the scope of remedies, and contradicts the requirements of Minnesota Statutes § 336.2-719, which calls for agreements limiting or modifying remedies to be explicitly stated and conspicuous. Section 1 is about confidential information, and linking remedies to that section is confusing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Sec. 336.2-719 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.2-719 336.2-718 336.2-720 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.2-719 Version List 336.2-719 CONTRACTUAL MODIFICATION OR LIMITATION OF REMEDY. (1) Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of nonconforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2) Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this chapter. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. History: 1965 c 811 s 336 .2-719 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw that creates a legal contradiction. By adding 'This is further related to section 1' to the remedies section, the modified text creates uncertainty about the scope of remedies, conflicting with the clarity required by Minnesota Statutes § 336.2-719, especially subsection (1)(a) which allows for remedies to be modified as long as the agreement explicitly states how. Linking remedies to a different section (Section 1, dealing with confidential information) muddies the clear rights and remedies available, and is therefore a structural flaw, per the prompt's definition. The added phrase undermines the clarity of the remedies section, because it does not explicitly modify the remedies.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes any prior or contemporaneous agreements or understandings, written or oral, concerning the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes any prior or contemporaneous agreements or understandings, written or oral, concerning the subject matter hereof. This applies only if section 2 is valid.
perturbation.explanation: Integration clauses are meant to say that the agreement is fully integrated. The new text reduces the clarity of the integration clause, adding uncertainty as to what is valid.
perturbation.law_explanation: By adding the phrase 'This applies only if section 2 is valid,' this modification contradicts Minnesota Statutes § 336.2-202, which dictates that fully integrated agreements are not to be contradicted by prior agreements or contemporaneous oral agreements. By suggesting that the entire agreement's validity hinges on Section 2, it undermines the entire agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Sec. 336.2-202 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.2-202 336.2-201 336.2-203 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.2-202 Version List Recent History 2024 336.2-202 Amended 2024 c 93 art 2 s 4 2004 336.2-202 Amended 2004 c 162 art 2 s 2 336.2-202 FINAL WRITTEN EXPRESSION; PAROL OR EXTRINSIC EVIDENCE. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a record intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented: (a) by course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade (section 336.1-303 ); and (b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the record to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. History: 1965 c 811 s 336 .2-202; 2004 c 162 art 2 s 2 ; 2024 c 93 art 2 s 4 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The added conditional phrase "This applies only if section 2 is valid" introduces uncertainty regarding the entire agreement's validity, especially the integration clause. Minnesota Statutes § 336.2-202 states that a final written agreement 'may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement.' By making the agreement's applicability contingent on Section 2's validity, the changed text undermines the agreement's finality and could allow for contradictions based on prior agreements if Section 2 is deemed invalid.
